- Check all restrictions to see if any (x, y) are now connected. If any restriction is violated (i.e., x and y are connected), then this request cannot be processed. So, we need to undo the union (rollback) and mark the request as false.

But for each request, checking all restrictions would take O(R) time, where R is the number of restrictions (up to 1000). Since there are up to 1000 requests, and each check is O(R), the total time would be O(R * Q). For R and Q up to 1000, that's 1,000,000 operations, which is acceptable.

Implement DSU:

class DSU {
public:
    vector<int> parent;
    vector<int> rank;
    DSU(int n) {
        parent.resize(n);
        rank.resize(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            parent[i] = i;
        }
    }
    int find(int x) {
        if (parent[x] != x) {
            parent[x] = find(parent[x]);
        }
        return parent[x];
    }
    bool unite(int x, int y) {
        int rootX = find(x);
        int rootY = find(y);
        if (rootX == rootY) return false;
        if (rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]) {
            parent[rootX] = rootY;
            rank[rootY]++;
        } else {
            parent[rootY] = rootX;
            rank[rootX]++;
        }
        return true;
    }
};

Then, in the friendRequests function:

vector<bool> friendRequests(int n, vector<vector<int>>& restrictions, vector<vector<int>>& requests) {
    DSU dsu(n);
    vector<bool> result;
    for (auto &req : requests) {
        int u = req[0];
        int v = req[1];
        bool success = true;
        if (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(v)) {
            // already connected
            success = true;
        } else {
            // check all restrictions
            for (auto &r : restrictions) {
                int x = r[0], y = r[1];
                if ( (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(x) && dsu.find(v) == dsu.find(y)) ||
                     (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(y) && dsu.find(v) == dsu.find(x)) ) {
                    success = false;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        if (success) {
            dsu.unite(u, v);
            result.push_back(true);
        } else {
            result.push_back(false);
        }
    }
    return result;
}

Now, code in C++.

But in the code, for each request, we check all restrictions. Now, the DSU is implemented as a helper class inside the Solution class? Or as a separate class. Since the starter code is a class Solution with the method, perhaps we can implement DSU as a nested class or use separate arrays.

But in C++, it's easier to implement DSU as a separate structure. Let me code it.

The code for the DSU is as follows.

Now, in the code:

The DSU is a class with parent and rank arrays. The find uses path compression, and unite uses union by rank.

In the friendRequests function, we create a DSU object with n. Then, for each request, process as discussed.

Now, code:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<bool> friendRequests(int n, vector<vector<int>>& restrictions, vector<vector<int>>& requests) {
        struct DSU {
            vector<int> parent;
            vector<int> rank;
            DSU(int n) {
                parent.resize(n);
                rank.resize(n);
                for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
                    parent[i] = i;
                }
            }
            int find(int x) {
                if (parent[x] != x) {
                    parent[x] = find(parent[x]);
                }
                return parent[x];
            }
            bool unite(int x, int y) {
                int rootX = find(x);
                int rootY = find(y);
                if (rootX == rootY) return false;
                if (rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]) {
                    parent[rootX] = rootY;
                    rank[rootY]++;
                } else {
                    parent[rootY] = rootX;
                    rank[rootX]++;
                }
                return true;
            }
        };
        vector<bool> result;
        DSU dsu(n);
        for (auto &req : requests) {
            int u = req[0];
            int v = req[1];
            bool success = true;
            if (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(v)) {
                // already connected
            } else {
                // check restrictions
                for (auto &r : restrictions) {
                    int x = r[0], y = r[1];
                    if ( (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(x) && dsu.find(v) == dsu.find(y)) ||
                         (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(y) && dsu.find(v) == dsu.find(x)) ) {
                        success = false;
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (success) {
                dsu.unite(u, v);
                result.push_back(true);
            } else {
                result.push_back(false);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Wait, but in the code above, the DSU is a struct inside the friendRequests function. But in C++, structs can be defined inside functions. So this should be okay.

But what about the time and space complexity?

Time complexity: For each request, we do O(α(n)) for find operations. For each restriction, we do two find operations. So for Q requests and R restrictions, the total time is O(Q * R * α(n)).

Space complexity is O(n) for the DSU.

Efficiency: HIGH? Well, for n up to 1e3, Q and R up to 1e3, this is acceptable. So HIGH.

Energy implications: LOW, since it's efficient.

But what about the example where the same component is checked multiple times? But the code is correct.

Then, merging them would connect x=u and y=v. So the code would check if (find(u) == find(u) and find(v) == find(v)), which is true. Hence, the restriction is violated. So the request is rejected. Which is correct.

Yes.

solution: ```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    vector<bool> friendRequests(int n, vector<vector<int>>& restrictions, vector<vector<int>>& requests) {
        struct DSU {
            vector<int> parent;
            vector<int> rank;
            DSU(int n) {
                parent.resize(n);
                rank.resize(n);
                for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
                    parent[i] = i;
                }
            }
            int find(int x) {
                if (parent[x] != x) {
                    parent[x] = find(parent[x]);
                }
                return parent[x];
            }
            bool unite(int x, int y) {
                int rootX = find(x);
                int rootY = find(y);
                if (rootX == rootY) return false;
                if (rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]) {
                    parent[rootX] = rootY;
                    rank[rootY]++;
                } else {
                    parent[rootY] = rootX;
                    rank[rootX]++;
                }
                return true;
            }
        };
        vector<bool> result;
        DSU dsu(n);
        for (auto &req : requests) {
            int u = req[0];
            int v = req[1];
            bool success = true;
            if (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(v)) {
                // already connected
            } else {
                for (auto &r : restrictions) {
                    int x = r[0], y = r[1];
                    if ((dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(x) && dsu.find(v) == dsu.find(y)) ||
                        (dsu.find(u) == dsu.find(y) && dsu.find(v) == dsu.find(x))) {
                        success = false;
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (success) {
                dsu.unite(u, v);
                result.push_back(true);
            } else {
                result.push_back(false);
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(Q * R * α(n))
space complexity: O(n + Q)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) structure with path compression and union by rank to efficiently manage connected components. For each friend request, it checks if merging the two nodes would violate any restrictions by comparing their current components against all restrictions. This approach ensures that each request is processed in near-constant time per restriction check, leading to high efficiency. The main weakness is the O(Q * R) factor, which could be a bottleneck for very large inputs, but remains acceptable within the problem constraints.
